43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) throughout. By a \emph{variety} over \(\bar k\)
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over \(\bar k\)
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over \(\bar k\).
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) defined on a dense open subset
54	\(U \subseteq X\), where two pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\), \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) are equivalent
55	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\) (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map \(f\)
56	is \emph{defined at} a point \(x \in X\) if there is some representative \((U, \varphi_U)\)
57	with \(x \in U\); the set of such \(x\) is an open subset \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X\),
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\).
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:
61	\begin{quote}
62	  \emph{When does a rational map \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) extend to a regular
63	  morphism on all of \(X\)?}
64	\end{quote}
65	Without hypotheses on \(X\) or \(Y\) the question has no positive answer (Milne lists three
66	counterexamples on p.~16: the inclusion of a proper open into the ambient variety; the
67	rational map to the cuspidal cubic whose inverse fails to extend; the inverse of a
68	blow-up at a smooth point). The two structural inputs that buy unconditional extension
69	in our setting are:
70	\begin{itemize}
71	  \item \emph{Target completeness:} if \(Y\) is complete (proper over \(\bar k\)), the
72	    valuative criterion of properness forces the rational map to extend across every
73	    codim-\(1\) point of a normal source \(X\) (Milne Theorem~3.1).
74	  \item \emph{Target group structure:} if \(Y\) is a group variety, the difference map
75	    \(\Phi(x, y) := f(x) \cdot f(y)^{-1}\) on \(X \times X\) has a vanishing/pole divisor
76	    along the diagonal that controls the codimension of the indeterminacy locus
77	    (Milne Lemma~3.3).
78	\end{itemize}
79	For \(Y\) an abelian variety both conditions hold, and combining them yields Milne's
80	Theorem~3.2 (the headline of \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}). The
81	present chapter packages the two inputs separately:
82	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension} is Milne~3.1, and
83	\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is Milne~3.3. The Weil-divisor reformulation
84	\cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} translates the codim-\(1\) extension obstruction
85	in Milne~3.1's proof into the order-at-a-prime-divisor language pinned in
86	\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, which is the form the Lean prover consumes when
87	working with the project's \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API.
88	
89	\section{The indeterminacy locus of a rational map}
90	\label{sec:indeterm_locus}
91	
92	\begin{definition}
93	
94	\leanok
95	  [Indeterminacy locus of a rational map]
96	  \label{def:indeterminacy_locus}
97	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus}
98	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S I.3, p.~16, the paragraph "Let \(V\) and \(W\)
99	  % be varieties over \(k\)\ldots" defining the domain of definition of a rational map
100	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
101	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi\) is said to be \emph{defined} at a point
102	  % \(v\) of \(V\) if \(v \in U\) for some \((U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi\). The set \(U_1\) of
103	  % \(v\) at which \(\varphi\) is defined is open, and there is a regular map
104	  % \(\varphi_1 \colon U_1 \to W\) such that \((U_1, \varphi_1) \in \varphi\) --- clearly,
105	  % \(U_1 = \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi} U\) and we can define \(\varphi_1\) to be
106	  % the regular map such that \(\varphi_1 | U = \varphi_U\) for all $(U, \varphi_U) \in
107	  % \varphi$."
108	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S I.3, p.~16.}
109	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
110	  The \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\) is the open set
111	  \[
112	    \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;:=\; \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in f} U \;\subseteq\; X,
113	  \]
114	  i.e.\ the union of the dense opens of all representatives of \(f\). The
115	  \emph{indeterminacy locus} of \(f\) is the closed complement
116	  \[
117	    Z(f) \;:=\; X \smallsetminus \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;\subseteq\; X.
118	  \]
119	  The map \(f\) is regular (i.e.\ defined on all of \(X\)) if and only if \(Z(f) = \emptyset\).
120	
121	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
122	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus} is a method on the
123	  Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure pinned at
124	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}; it returns a
125	  \texttt{Set X} (the underlying carrier of the closed complement of
126	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.domain}, which is already exposed by Mathlib). The
127	  closedness of \(Z(f)\) is a separate \texttt{IsClosed} witness, packaged either as a
128	  field of \texttt{indeterminacyLocus} or as a standalone lemma
129	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.isClosed\_indeterminacyLocus}, whichever is cleaner with
130	  Mathlib's existing API at commit \texttt{b80f227}.
131	\end{definition}

140	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 --- the conclusion
141	  % "an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)"
142	  % is the standing predicate we abstract (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
143	  % PDF page 22).
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow W\) from a normal
145	  % variety \(V\) to a complete variety \(W\) is defined on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose
146	  % complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)."
147	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 (the
148	  conclusion).}
149	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
150	  We say \(f\) is \emph{codim-1-indeterminacy-free} (or has \emph{codimension-\(\geq 2\)
151	  indeterminacy}) if every irreducible component of the closed subset \(Z(f) \subseteq X\)
152	  has codimension \(\geq 2\) in \(X\). Equivalently, no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in
153	  \(Z(f)\); equivalently, every point \(\eta \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\)
154	  lies in \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\).

156	  \paragraph{Why the abstraction.} Milne's Theorem~3.1 (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}
157	  below) and Lemma~3.3 (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} below) both produce
158	  conclusions about the codimension of \(Z(f)\). Phrasing the codim-\(\geq 2\) condition
159	  as a named predicate keeps the cited statements concise and lets the consumer
160	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} read off the combination
161	  (\(Z(f)\) has codim \(\geq 2\) from \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} \emph{and} \(Z(f)\) is
162	  empty or pure codim 1 from \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} \(\Rightarrow\) \(Z(f)\)
163	  is empty) as a single line.
164	
165	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
166	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree} is a \texttt{Prop}-valued
167	  predicate, encoded via Mathlib's \texttt{Order.coheight} on the specialisation
168	  preorder of \texttt{X.carrier} as in
169	  \cref{def:prime_divisor}: \(f\) is codim-1-indeterminacy-free if and only if for every
170	  \(\eta \in X\) with \(\texttt{Order.coheight\ }\eta = 1\), $\eta \in
171	  \texttt{f.domain}\(. The encoding matches the standing \)\mathrm{Order.coheight}$
172	  convention pinned in \cref{def:prime_divisor}, so prime divisors of \(X\) and the
173	  hypothesis ``no codim-\(1\) indeterminacy'' use the same Mathlib idiom.
174	\end{definition}

176	\section{Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point}
177	\label{sec:smooth_dvr}
178	
179	The codim-\(1\) extension argument in Milne's proof of Theorem~3.1 hinges on the local
180	ring at the generic point of a prime divisor being a discrete valuation ring (so that
181	the valuative criterion of properness applies). On a normal variety this is the
182	defining property of regularity in codimension one; on a smooth variety it is automatic.
183	
184	The smooth-implies-regular-local-stalk implication itself is the Jacobian-criterion
185	direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}; we package it as a separate sub-lemma so that
186	\cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} below reads cleanly as ``regular local of dimension
187	\(1\) is a DVR'', with the regularity prerequisite supplied by the sub-lemma.
188

459	  \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. Then the
460	  indeterminacy locus \(Z(f)\) (\cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}) has codimension

508	  of properness applied to the complete variety \(Y\)
509	  (\emph{cf.}\ Hartshorne~II.4.7, cited verbatim in Milne~3.1's proof), this morphism
510	  extends to a morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}) \to Y\). Standard
511	  algebraic-geometry spreading then exhibits an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(\eta\) in
512	  \(X\) on which \(f\) has a regular representative; in particular $\eta \in
513	  \mathrm{Dom}(f)\(, contradicting \)\eta \in W \subseteq Z(f)\(. Hence no codim-\)1$
514	  component of \(X\) lies in \(Z(f)\), i.e.\ \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\).
515	
516	  \emph{Step 2: extend across codim-\(\geq 2\) points (the "codim-1-indeterminacy-free
517	  \(\Rightarrow\) extension" half).} Suppose now that \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)
518	  but is non-empty (we want to deduce \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) under the additional input
519	  that ``\(f\) is already codim-1-indeterminacy-free, by hypothesis''). Pick a closed
520	  point \(x \in Z(f)\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} \geq 2\). By
521	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} applied to the regular local ring
522	  \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\), we have
523	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = \dim(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\). The sheaf
524	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on the punctured neighbourhood \(V \smallsetminus \{x\}\)
525	  (any small affine neighbourhood \(V \ni x\)) has \(H^0_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\)
526	  and \(H^1_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\) (the depth-\(\geq 2\) characterisation of
527	  local-cohomology vanishing, Stacks tag \texttt{0AVF}; cf.\ Hartshorne~III.3.5 for
528	  the surface case). Pull back the coordinates of any affine chart of \(Y\) around the
529	  closure \(\overline{f(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \cap V)}\) along \(f\); the resulting sections of
530	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(V \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) extend uniquely to sections on \(V\),
531	  yielding a regular representative of \(f\) on \(V\). Hence \(x \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\),
532	  contradicting \(x \in Z(f)\). Therefore \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) and \(f\) extends to a
533	  regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to Y\).
534	
535	  Uniqueness of \(\widetilde f\) is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement
536	  principle: two morphisms \(X \to Y\) that agree on the dense open \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\)
537	  agree on all of \(X\).
538	\end{proof}
539	
540	\begin{remark}
541	  \label{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}
542	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} Step~1 of the proof uses only normality
543	  + the valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne~II.4.7) and is characteristic-free.
544	  Step~2 uses the depth-\(\geq 2\) extension property at a codim-\(\geq 2\) regular
545	  point, which is exactly what \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of
546	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies (regular local of dimension \(d\)
547	  \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(d\)). For our application the source \(X\) is a
548	  smooth variety, so the regular-local-ring hypothesis is automatic; the Cohen--Macaulay
549	  consequence is what feeds the local-cohomology vanishing.
550	
551	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
552	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), the codim-\(\geq 2\) closed points of
553	  \(X\) are exactly the closed points of \(X\) (because \(\dim X = 2\)), and at each such
554	  point \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular of dimension \(2\), hence Cohen--Macaulay of
555	  depth \(2\). The smooth-surface case is the one A.4.c will invoke when extending the
556	  symmetric-product rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) along the Abel--Jacobi
557	  birational equivalence to \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\).
558	\end{remark}

560	\section{Milne Lemma 3.3: indeterminacy locus of a map to a group variety is codim 1}
561	\label{sec:milne_lem33}
562	
563	The second input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is structural: when the target \(Y\) is a group
564	variety, the indeterminacy locus is either empty or of \emph{pure} codimension \(1\).
565	Combined with \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s ``codimension \(\geq 2\)'' conclusion,
566	this forces the locus to be empty.

629	  
630	  Following Milne's proof of Lemma~3.3. The argument has four sub-steps. Throughout we
631	  fix a representative \((U, \varphi_U)\) of \(f\).
632	
633	  \emph{Sub-step 1: the difference rational map.} Define
634	  \[
635	    \Phi \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow G,
636	    \qquad
637	    \Phi(x, y) \;:=\; \varphi(x) \cdot \varphi(y)^{-1},
638	  \]
639	  via the composition
640	  \[
641	    U \times U
642	    \;\xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U}\; G \times G
643	    \;\xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}}\; G \times G
644	    \;\xrightarrow{m}\; G,

647	  \(m \colon G \times G \to G\) is the group law. The map \(\Phi\) is rational by
648	  composition: its domain is at least \(U \times U \subseteq X \times X\), which is dense
649	  because \(U\) is dense in \(X\).

652	  \(x\).} If \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\), the explicit formula \(\Phi(x, x) = e\) (the
653	  identity of \(G\)) shows \(\Phi\) is defined at \((x, x)\). Conversely, if \(\Phi\) is
654	  defined at \((x, x)\), openness of \(\mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)\) in \(X \times X\) produces an
655	  open set \(W \ni (x, x)\) on which \(\Phi\) is defined; by projection (taking the slice
656	  \(\{x\} \times W' \subseteq W\) for some open \(W' \subseteq X\), possibly shrinking
657	  \(W'\) so \(\varphi\) is defined on \(W'\) as well --- \(W'\) need not contain \(x\)), the
658	  identity

661	    \qquad u \in W',

664	  \mathrm{Dom}(\varphi)\( if and only if \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)$.
665	
666	  \emph{Sub-step 3: rephrase via pullback of local rings.} The rational map \(\Phi\)
667	  pulls \(\mathcal O_{G, e}\) back to a \(\bar k\)-subalgebra of the function field
668	  \(K(X \times X)\):
669	  \[
670	    \Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \;\longrightarrow\; K(X \times X).
671	  \]
672	  Because \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) wherever it is defined, \(\Phi\) is defined at
673	  \((x, x)\) if and only if $\Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subseteq \mathcal O_{X
674	  \times X, (x, x)}\( (i.e.\ every local function on \)G\( near \)e$ has no pole at
675	  \((x, x)\) after pullback).

677	  \emph{Sub-step 4: pole locus is pure codimension \(1\).} The variety \(X \times X\) is
678	  nonsingular (smoothness is stable under products over \(\bar k\)), so the Weil-divisor
679	  apparatus of \cref{def:codim1_cycles} and the order map of
680	  \cref{def:order_at_point} apply on \(X \times X\). For any nonzero rational function
681	  \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) decompose its divisor as
682	  \[
683	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty,
684	  \]
685	  with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) effective and
686	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = \mathrm{div}(f^{-1})_0\) (Milne's notation, matching the
687	  project's \(\div(f) = \sum \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y\) of
688	  \cref{def:principal_divisor}: \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) collects the prime divisors with

690	  ring at \((x, x)\) can be characterised as
691	  \[
692	    \mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}
693	    \;=\; \bigl\{\, f \in K(X \times X) \;\big|\;
694	    \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x) \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
695	  \]
696	  Suppose \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) is not in \(\mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}\);
697	  equivalently, \((x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty)\). The pole divisor
698	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) is a finite sum of prime divisors of \(X \times X\) (i.e.\ a
699	  closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X \times X\)). Its intersection with the
700	  diagonal \(\Delta_X \subseteq X \times X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\)
701	  in \(\Delta_X\) (this is Milne's reference to AG~9.2, the standard fact that a prime
702	  divisor of \(X \times X\) either contains \(\Delta_X\) or meets it in pure codimension
703	  \(1\); on a nonsingular variety this is an instance of Krull's principal-ideal theorem
704	  applied to the local equation of \(\Delta_X\) inside \(X \times X\)).

706	  \emph{Conclusion.} Suppose \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow G\) is not defined on all of
707	  \(X\). Pick \(x \in Z(f)\). By Sub-steps~2--3, there is some $f_0 \in
708	  \Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e})\( with \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty)$.

710	  \Delta_X\( is pure codimension \)1\( in \)\Delta_X\(, passing through \)(x, x)$.
711	  Identifying \(\Delta_X\) with \(X\) via the diagonal isomorphism, the corresponding
712	  closed subset of \(X\) has pure codimension \(1\) and passes through \(x\). By Sub-step~2,

714	  diagonal, hence where \(\varphi\) is undefined. Thus \(Z(\varphi)\) contains a pure
715	  codim-\(1\) closed subset passing through \(x\); since \(x \in Z(\varphi)\) was arbitrary,
716	  every irreducible component of \(Z(\varphi)\) has codimension exactly \(1\), i.e.\
717	  \(Z(\varphi)\) is of pure codimension \(1\).

730	  lemma in this generality at \S I.3, p.~17.)

856	  \label{lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle}
857	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point}
858	  Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be varieties over \(\bar k\) and let
859	  \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. For a prime divisor
860	  \(W \subseteq X\) with generic point \(\eta_W = W.\mathrm{point}\), the
861	  following are equivalent:
862	  \begin{enumerate}
863	    \item \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) (\(f\) is defined at the generic point of
864	      \(W\)).
865	    \item There exists a partial-map representative \((U, \varphi_U)\) of \(f\) ---
866	      i.e.\ a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) on a dense open
867	      \(U \subseteq X\) whose induced rational map equals \(f\) --- whose domain
868	      \(U\) contains \(\eta_W\).
869	  \end{enumerate}
870	\end{lemma}

877	  \(f\) defined at \(\eta_W\); the only content here is reordering the two
878	  conjuncts under the existential so the \(\varphi_U \mapsto f\) component lands
879	  first (matching the way later witnesses in the project construct partial-map
880	  representatives). The forward and backward implications are immediate from
881	  the unfolded definition.
882	\end{proof}

728	indeterminacy locus either empty or of pure codimension `1`. Combined with

735	codim 1.**
736	
737	For `X` a nonsingular variety over `k̄`, `G` a group variety over `k̄`
738	(a smooth group scheme of finite type, separated), and `f : X ⇢ G` a
739	rational map, the indeterminacy locus `Z(f) := indeterminacyLocus f` is
740	either empty (`f` is defined on all of `X`) or every point of `Z(f)` lies in
741	the closure of a codim-1 generic point.
742	
743	The proof (Milne's): consider the difference map
744	`Φ : X × X ⇢ G`, `(x, y) ↦ f(x) · f(y)⁻¹`. Then `Φ` is defined at `(x, x)`
745	iff `f` is defined at `x`. Pulling back the maximal ideal of `O_{G,e}` gives
746	a finite set of rational functions on `X × X` whose pole divisors are pure
747	codim-1; their intersection with the diagonal exhibits the indeterminacy
748	locus of `f` as pure codim-1.

38452	\section{Smooth algebras over fields}
38453	\label{section-smooth-over-field}
38454	
38455	\noindent
38456	Warning: The following two lemmas do not hold over nonperfect
38457	fields in general.
38458	
38459	\begin{lemma}
38460	\label{lemma-rank-omega}
38461	Let $k$ be an algebraically closed field.
38462	Let $S$ be a finite type $k$-algebra.
38463	Let $\mathfrak m \subset S$ be a maximal ideal.
38464	Then
38465	$$
38466	\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak m)} \Omega_{S/k} \otimes_S \kappa(\mathfrak m)
38467	=
38468	\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak m)} \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2.
38469	$$
38470	\end{lemma}
38471	
38472	\begin{proof}
38473	Consider the exact sequence
38474	$$
38475	\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 \to
38476	\Omega_{S/k} \otimes_S \kappa(\mathfrak m) \to
38477	\Omega_{\kappa(\mathfrak m)/k} \to 0
38478	$$
38479	of Lemma \ref{lemma-differential-seq}. We would like to show that the
38480	first map is an isomorphism. Since $k$ is algebraically closed the
38481	composition $k \to \kappa(\mathfrak m)$ is an isomorphism by
38482	Theorem \ref{theorem-nullstellensatz}.
38483	So the surjection $S \to \kappa(\mathfrak m)$ splits as a map of
38484	$k$-algebras, and Lemma \ref{lemma-differential-seq-split} shows
38485	that the sequence above is exact
38486	on the left. Since $\Omega_{\kappa(\mathfrak m)/k} = 0$, we win.
38487	\end{proof}
38488	
38489	\begin{lemma}
38490	\label{lemma-characterize-smooth-kbar}
38491	Let $k$ be an algebraically closed field.
38492	Let $S$ be a finite type $k$-algebra.
38493	Let $\mathfrak m \subset S$ be a maximal ideal.
38494	The following are equivalent:
38495	\begin{enumerate}
38496	\item The ring $S_{\mathfrak m}$ is a regular local ring.
38497	\item We have
38498	$\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak m)} \Omega_{S/k} \otimes_S \kappa(\mathfrak m)
38499	\leq \dim(S_{\mathfrak m})$.
38500	\item We have
38501	$\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak m)} \Omega_{S/k} \otimes_S \kappa(\mathfrak m)
38502	= \dim(S_{\mathfrak m})$.
38503	\item There exists a $g \in S$, $g \not \in \mathfrak m$
38504	such that $S_g$ is smooth over $k$. In other words $S/k$
38505	is smooth at $\mathfrak m$.
38506	\end{enumerate}
38507	\end{lemma}
38508	
38509	\begin{proof}
38510	Note that (1), (2) and (3) are equivalent by Lemma \ref{lemma-rank-omega}
38511	and Definition \ref{definition-regular}.
38512	
38513	\medskip\noindent
38514	Assume that $S$ is smooth at $\mathfrak m$.
38515	By Lemma \ref{lemma-smooth-syntomic} we see that
38516	$S_g$ is standard smooth over $k$
38517	for a suitable $g \in S$, $g \not \in \mathfrak m$.
38518	Hence by Lemma \ref{lemma-standard-smooth}
38519	we see that $\Omega_{S_g/k}$ is free of rank $\dim(S_g)$.
38520	Hence by Lemma \ref{lemma-rank-omega}
38521	we see that $\dim(S_{\mathfrak m}) = \dim (\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2)$
38522	in other words $S_\mathfrak m$ is regular.
38523	
38524	\medskip\noindent
38525	Conversely, suppose that $S_{\mathfrak m}$ is regular.
38526	Let $d = \dim(S_{\mathfrak m}) = \dim \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2$.
38527	Choose a presentation $S = k[x_1, \ldots, x_n]/I$
38528	such that $x_i$ maps to an element of $\mathfrak m$ for
38529	all $i$. In other words, $\mathfrak m'' = (x_1, \ldots, x_n)$
38530	is the corresponding maximal ideal of $k[x_1, \ldots, x_n]$.
38531	Note that we have a short exact sequence
38532	$$
38533	I/\mathfrak m''I \to \mathfrak m''/(\mathfrak m'')^2
38534	\to \mathfrak m/(\mathfrak m)^2 \to 0
38535	$$
38536	Pick $c = n - d$ elements $f_1, \ldots, f_c \in I$ such that
38537	their images in $\mathfrak m''/(\mathfrak m'')^2$ span the
38538	kernel of the map to $\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2$. This is clearly
38539	possible. Denote $J = (f_1, \ldots, f_c)$. So $J \subset I$.
38540	Denote $S' = k[x_1, \ldots, x_n]/J$ so there is a surjection
38541	$S' \to S$. Denote $\mathfrak m' = \mathfrak m''S'$ the corresponding
38542	maximal ideal of $S'$. Hence we have
38543	$$
38544	\xymatrix{
38545	k[x_1, \ldots, x_n] \ar[r] & S' \ar[r] & S \\
38546	\mathfrak m'' \ar[u] \ar[r] & \mathfrak m' \ar[r] \ar[u] &
38547	\mathfrak m \ar[u]
38548	}
38549	$$
38550	By our choice of $J$ the exact sequence
38551	$$
38552	J/\mathfrak m''J \to \mathfrak m''/(\mathfrak m'')^2
38553	\to \mathfrak m'/(\mathfrak m')^2 \to 0
38554	$$
38555	shows that $\dim( \mathfrak m'/(\mathfrak m')^2 ) = d$.
38556	Since $S'_{\mathfrak m'}$ surjects onto $S_{\mathfrak m}$
38557	we see that $\dim(S_{\mathfrak m'}) \geq d$. Hence by
38558	the discussion preceding Definition \ref{definition-regular-local}
38559	we conclude that $S'_{\mathfrak m'}$ is
38560	regular of dimension $d$ as well. Because $S'$ was cut out
38561	by $c = n - d$ equations we
38562	conclude that there exists a $g' \in S'$, $g' \not \in \mathfrak m'$
38563	such that $S'_{g'}$ is a global complete intersection over $k$,
38564	see Lemma \ref{lemma-lci}.
38565	Also the map $S'_{\mathfrak m'} \to S_{\mathfrak m}$
38566	is a surjection of Noetherian local domains of the same
38567	dimension and hence an isomorphism. Hence $S' \to S$ is surjective
38568	with finitely generated kernel and becomes an isomorphism
38569	after localizing at $\mathfrak m'$. Thus we can find $g' \in S'$,
38570	$g \not \in \mathfrak m'$ such that $S'_{g'} \to S_{g'}$
38571	is an isomorphism. All in all we conclude that
38572	after replacing $S$ by a principal localization we may
38573	assume that $S$ is a global complete intersection.
38574	
38575	\medskip\noindent
38576	At this point we may write $S = k[x_1, \ldots, x_n]/(f_1, \ldots, f_c)$
38577	with $\dim S = n - c$. Recall that the naive cotangent complex
38578	of this algebra is given by
38579	$$
38580	\bigoplus S \cdot f_j
38581	\to
38582	\bigoplus S \cdot \text{d}x_i
38583	$$
38584	see Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-global-complete-intersection-conormal}.
38585	By Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-global-complete-intersection-smooth}
38586	in order to show that $S$ is smooth at
38587	$\mathfrak m$ we have to show that one of the $c \times c$
38588	minors $g_I$ of the matrix ``$A$'' giving the map above
38589	does not vanish at $\mathfrak m$. By Lemma \ref{lemma-rank-omega}
38590	the matrix $A \bmod \mathfrak m$ has rank $c$. Thus we win.
38591	\end{proof}
38592	
38593	\begin{lemma}
38594	\label{lemma-characterize-smooth-over-field}
38595	Let $k$ be any field.
38596	Let $S$ be a finite type $k$-algebra.
38597	Let $X = \Spec(S)$.
38598	Let $\mathfrak q \subset S$ be a prime
38599	corresponding to $x \in X$.
38600	The following are equivalent:
38601	\begin{enumerate}
38602	\item The $k$-algebra $S$ is smooth at $\mathfrak q$ over $k$.
38603	\item We have
38604	$\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \Omega_{S/k} \otimes_S \kappa(\mathfrak q)
38605	\leq \dim_x X$.
38606	\item We have
38607	$\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \Omega_{S/k} \otimes_S \kappa(\mathfrak q)
38608	= \dim_x X$.
38609	\end{enumerate}
38610	Moreover, in this case the local ring $S_{\mathfrak q}$ is regular.
38611	\end{lemma}
38612	
38613	\begin{proof}
38614	If $S$ is smooth at $\mathfrak q$ over $k$, then there exists
38615	a $g \in S$, $g \not \in \mathfrak q$ such that $S_g$ is
38616	standard smooth over $k$, see Lemma \ref{lemma-smooth-syntomic}.
38617	A standard smooth algebra over $k$ has a module of differentials
38618	which is free of rank equal to the dimension, see
38619	Lemma \ref{lemma-standard-smooth} (use that a relative global
38620	complete intersection over a field has dimension equal to the
38621	number of variables minus the number of equations). Thus we see that
38622	(1) implies (3). To finish the proof of the lemma it
38623	suffices to show that (2) implies (1) and that it implies
38624	that $S_{\mathfrak q}$ is regular.
38625	
38626	\medskip\noindent
38627	Assume (2). By Nakayama's Lemma \ref{lemma-NAK} we see that
38628	$\Omega_{S/k, \mathfrak q}$ can be generated by $\leq \dim_x X$ elements.
38629	We may replace $S$ by $S_g$ for some $g \in S$, $g \not \in \mathfrak q$
38630	such that $\Omega_{S/k}$ is generated by at most
38631	$\dim_x X$ elements.
38632	Let $K/k$ be an algebraically closed field extension
38633	such that there exists a $k$-algebra map $\psi : \kappa(\mathfrak q) \to K$.
38634	Consider $S_K = K \otimes_k S$. Let $\mathfrak m \subset S_K$
38635	be the maximal ideal corresponding to the surjection
38636	$$
38637	\xymatrix{
38638	S_K = K \otimes_k S \ar[r] &
38639	K \otimes_k \kappa(\mathfrak q)
38640	\ar[r]^-{\text{id}_K \otimes \psi} &
38641	K.
38642	}
38643	$$
38644	Note that $\mathfrak m \cap S = \mathfrak q$, in other words
38645	$\mathfrak m$ lies over $\mathfrak q$.
38646	By Lemma \ref{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-preserved-field-extension}
38647	the dimension of $X_K = \Spec(S_K)$ at the point corresponding
38648	to $\mathfrak m$ is $\dim_x X$. By
38649	Lemma \ref{lemma-dimension-closed-point-finite-type-field}

38737	\begin{proof}
38738	Denote $R = S_{\mathfrak q}$ and denote its maximal
38739	by $\mathfrak m$ and its residue field $\kappa$.
38740	By Lemma \ref{lemma-computation-differential} and
38741	\ref{lemma-differential-seq} we see that there is a short exact

38745	\Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa \to
38746	\Omega_{\kappa/k} \to 0
38747	$$
38748	Note that $\Omega_{R/k} = \Omega_{S/k, \mathfrak q}$, see
38749	Lemma \ref{lemma-differentials-localize}.
38750	Moreover, since $\kappa$ is separable over $k$
38751	we have $\dim_{\kappa} \Omega_{\kappa/k} = \text{trdeg}_k(\kappa)$.
38752	Hence we get
38753	$$
38754	\dim_{\kappa} \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa
38755	=
38756	\dim_\kappa \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 + \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa)
38757	\geq

38759	=

38761	$$
38762	(see Lemma \ref{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field} for

38764	with equality if and only if $R$ is regular.
38765	Thus we win by applying Lemma \ref{lemma-characterize-smooth-over-field}.

38768	\begin{lemma}
38769	\label{lemma-characteristic-zero}
38770	Let $R \to S$ be a $\mathbf{Q}$-algebra map.
38771	Let $f \in S$ be such that $\Omega_{S/R} = S \text{d}f \oplus C$
38772	for some $S$-submodule $C$. Then
38773	\begin{enumerate}
38774	\item $f$ is not nilpotent, and
38775	\item if $S$ is a Noetherian local ring, then $f$ is a nonzerodivisor in $S$.
38776	\end{enumerate}
38777	\end{lemma}
38778	
38779	\begin{proof}
38780	For $a \in S$ write $\text{d}(a) = \theta(a)\text{d}f + c(a)$ for some
38781	$\theta(a) \in S$ and $c(a) \in C$.
38782	Consider the $R$-derivation $S \to S$, $a \mapsto \theta(a)$.
38783	Note that $\theta(f) = 1$.
38784	
38785	\medskip\noindent
38786	If $f^n = 0$ with $n > 1$ minimal, then $0 = \theta(f^n) = n f^{n - 1}$
38787	contradicting the minimality of $n$. We conclude that $f$ is not nilpotent.
38788	
38789	\medskip\noindent
38790	Suppose $fa = 0$. If $f$ is a unit then $a = 0$ and we win. Assume
38791	$f$ is not a unit. Then
38792	$0 = \theta(fa) = f\theta(a) + a$ by the Leibniz rule and hence $a \in (f)$.
38793	By induction suppose we have shown $fa = 0 \Rightarrow a \in (f^n)$.
38794	Then writing $a = f^nb$ we get
38795	$0 = \theta(f^{n + 1}b) = (n + 1)f^nb + f^{n + 1}\theta(b)$.
38796	Hence $a = f^n b = -f^{n + 1}\theta(b)/(n + 1) \in (f^{n + 1})$.
38797	Since in the Noetherian local ring $S$ we have $\bigcap (f^n) = 0$, see
38798	Lemma \ref{lemma-intersect-powers-ideal-module-zero}
38799	we win.
38800	\end{proof}

38807	Let $k$ be a field of characteristic $0$.
38808	Let $S$ be a finite type $k$-algebra.

38813	\item The $S_{\mathfrak q}$-module $\Omega_{S/k, \mathfrak q}$
38814	is (finite) free.
38815	\item The ring $S_{\mathfrak q}$ is regular.
38816	\end{enumerate}
38817	\end{lemma}
38818	
38819	\begin{proof}

38821	equivalence of (1) and (3) follows from Lemma \ref{lemma-separable-smooth}.
38822	Also (1) implies (2) by definition of smooth algebras.
38823	Assume that $\Omega_{S/k, \mathfrak q}$ is free over $S_{\mathfrak q}$.
38824	We are going to use the notation and observations made in the
38825	proof of Lemma \ref{lemma-separable-smooth}. So $R = S_{\mathfrak q}$
38826	with maximal ideal $\mathfrak m$ and residue field $\kappa$.
38827	Our goal is to prove $R$ is regular.
38828	
38829	\medskip\noindent
38830	If $\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 = 0$, then $\mathfrak m = 0$
38831	and $R \cong \kappa$. Hence $R$ is regular and we win.

38833	\medskip\noindent
38834	If $\mathfrak m/ \mathfrak m^2 \not = 0$, then choose any
38835	$f \in \mathfrak m$ whose image in $\mathfrak m/ \mathfrak m^2$
38836	is not zero. By Lemma \ref{lemma-computation-differential}
38837	we see that $\text{d}f$ has nonzero image in
38838	$\Omega_{R/k}/\mathfrak m\Omega_{R/k}$. By assumption
38839	$\Omega_{R/k} = \Omega_{S/k, \mathfrak q}$ is finite free and
38840	hence by Nakayama's Lemma \ref{lemma-NAK} we see that
38841	$\text{d}f$ generates a direct summand. We apply
38842	Lemma \ref{lemma-characteristic-zero}
38843	to deduce that $f$ is a nonzerodivisor in $R$.
38844	Furthermore, by Lemma \ref{lemma-differential-seq} we get an exact sequence
38845	$$
38846	(f)/(f^2) \to \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R R/fR \to \Omega_{(R/fR)/k} \to 0
38847	$$
38848	This implies that $\Omega_{(R/fR)/k}$ is finite free as well.
38849	Hence by induction we see that $R/fR$ is a regular local ring.
38850	Since $f \in \mathfrak m$ was a nonzerodivisor we
38851	conclude that $R$ is regular, see Lemma \ref{lemma-regular-mod-x}.
38852	\end{proof}
38853	
38854	\begin{example}
38855	\label{example-characteristic-p}
38856	Lemma \ref{lemma-characteristic-zero-local-smooth}
38857	does not hold in characteristic $p > 0$.
38858	The standard examples are the ring maps
38859	$$
38860	\mathbf{F}_p \longrightarrow \mathbf{F}_p[x]/(x^p)
38861	$$
38862	whose module of differentials is free but is clearly not smooth, and
38863	the ring map ($p > 2$)
38864	$$
38865	\mathbf{F}_p(t) \to \mathbf{F}_p(t)[x, y]/(x^p + y^2 + \alpha)
38866	$$
38867	which is not smooth at the prime $\mathfrak q = (y, x^p + \alpha)$
38868	but is regular.
38869	\end{example}

38871	\noindent
38872	Using the material above we can characterize smoothness at the generic
38873	point in terms of field extensions.
38874	
38875	\begin{lemma}
38876	\label{lemma-smooth-at-generic-point}
38877	Let $R \to S$ be an injective finite type ring map with $R$ and $S$ domains.
38878	Then $R \to S$ is smooth at $\mathfrak q = (0)$ if and only if
38879	the induced extension $L/K$ of fraction fields is separable.
38880	\end{lemma}
38881	
38882	\begin{proof}
38883	Assume $R \to S$ is smooth at $(0)$. We may replace $S$ by $S_g$
38884	for some nonzero $g \in S$ and assume that $R \to S$ is smooth.
38885	Then $K \to S \otimes_R K$ is smooth
38886	(Lemma \ref{lemma-base-change-smooth}). Moreover, for any
38887	field extension $K'/K$ the ring map $K' \to S \otimes_R K'$
38888	is smooth as well. Hence $S \otimes_R K'$ is a regular ring
38889	by Lemma \ref{lemma-characterize-smooth-over-field}, in particular reduced.
38890	It follows that $S \otimes_R K$ is a geometrically reduced over $K$.
38891	Hence $L$ is geometrically reduced over $K$, see
38892	Lemma \ref{lemma-geometrically-reduced-permanence}.
38893	Hence $L/K$ is separable by
38894	Lemma \ref{lemma-characterize-separable-field-extensions}.
38895	
38896	\medskip\noindent
38897	Conversely, assume that $L/K$ is separable.
38898	We may assume $R \to S$ is of finite presentation, see
38899	Lemma \ref{lemma-generic-finite-presentation}.
38900	It suffices to prove that $K \to S \otimes_R K$ is smooth
38901	at $(0)$, see
38902	Lemma \ref{lemma-flat-base-change-locus-smooth}.
38903	This follows from Lemma \ref{lemma-separable-smooth}, the
38904	fact that a field is a regular ring,
38905	and the assumption that $L/K$ is separable.
38906	\end{proof}

38941	\item $R \to S$ is smooth at $\mathfrak q$,
38942	\item for every surjection of local $R$-algebras
38943	$(B', \mathfrak m') \to (B, \mathfrak m)$
38944	with $\Ker(B' \to B)$ having square zero
38945	and every solid commutative diagram
38946	$$
38947	\xymatrix{
38948	S \ar[r] \ar@{-->}[rd] & B \\
38949	R \ar[r] \ar[u] & B' \ar[u]
38950	}
38951	$$
38952	such that $\mathfrak q = S \cap \mathfrak m$ there exists a dotted
38953	arrow making the diagram commute,
38954	\item same as in (2) but with $B' \to B$ ranging over small extensions, and
38955	\item same as in (2) but with $B' \to B$ ranging over small extensions
38956	such that in addition $S \to B$ induces an isomorphism
38957	$\kappa(\mathfrak q) \cong \kappa(\mathfrak m)$.
38958	\end{enumerate}
38959	\end{lemma}
38960	
38961	\begin{proof}
38962	Assume (1). This means there exists a $g \in S$, $g \not \in \mathfrak q$
38963	such that $R \to S_g$ is smooth. By
38964	Proposition \ref{proposition-smooth-formally-smooth}
38965	we know that $R \to S_g$ is formally smooth. Note that given any diagram
38966	as in (2) the map $S \to B$ factors automatically through $S_{\mathfrak q}$
38967	and a fortiori through $S_g$. The formal smoothness of $S_g$ over $R$
38968	gives us a morphism $S_g \to B'$ fitting into a similar diagram with $S_g$ at
38969	the upper left corner. Composing with $S \to S_g$ gives the desired arrow.

28202	\noindent
28203	The following lemma generalizes
28204	Lemma \ref{lemma-dimension-closed-point-finite-type-field}.

28206	\begin{lemma}
28207	\label{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}
28208	Let $k$ be a field.
28209	Let $S$ be a finite type $k$ algebra.
28210	Let $X = \Spec(S)$.
28211	Let $\mathfrak p \subset S$ be a prime ideal,
28212	and let $x \in X$ be the corresponding point.
28213	Then we have
28214	$$
28215	\dim_x(X) = \dim(S_{\mathfrak p}) + \text{trdeg}_k\ \kappa(\mathfrak p).
28216	$$
28217	\end{lemma}
28218	
28219	\begin{proof}
28220	By Lemma \ref{lemma-dimension-prime-polynomial-ring} we know that
28221	$r = \text{trdeg}_k\ \kappa(\mathfrak p)$ is equal to the
28222	dimension of $V(\mathfrak p)$.
28223	Pick any maximal chain of primes
28224	$\mathfrak p \subset \mathfrak p_1 \subset \ldots \subset \mathfrak p_r$
28225	starting with $\mathfrak p$ in $S$.
28226	This has length $r$ by Lemma \ref{lemma-dimension-spell-it-out}.
28227	Let $\mathfrak q_j$, $j \in J$ be the minimal
28228	primes of $S$ which are contained in $\mathfrak p$.
28229	These correspond $1-1$ to minimal primes in $S_{\mathfrak p}$
28230	via the rule $\mathfrak q_j \mapsto \mathfrak q_jS_{\mathfrak p}$.
28231	By Lemma \ref{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-over-field}
28232	we know that $\dim_x(X)$ is equal
28233	to the maximum of the dimensions of the rings $S/\mathfrak q_j$.
28234	For each $j$ pick a maximal chain of primes
28235	$\mathfrak q_j \subset \mathfrak p'_1 \subset \ldots \subset \mathfrak p'_{s(j)}
28236	= \mathfrak p$.
28237	Then $\dim(S_{\mathfrak p}) = \max_{j \in J} s(j)$.
28238	Now, each chain
28239	$$
28240	\mathfrak q_j \subset \mathfrak p'_1 \subset \ldots \subset
28241	\mathfrak p'_{s(j)} = \mathfrak p \subset
28242	\mathfrak p_1 \subset \ldots \subset \mathfrak p_r
28243	$$
28244	is a maximal chain in $S/\mathfrak q_j$, and by what was said
28245	before we have
28246	$\dim_x(X) = \max_{j \in J} r + s(j)$.
28247	The lemma follows.
28248	\end{proof}

28254	\begin{lemma}

34062	\begin{lemma}
34063	\label{lemma-differentials-localize}
34064	Let $\varphi : A \to B$ be a ring map.
34065	\begin{enumerate}
34066	\item If $S \subset A$ is a multiplicative subset mapping to
34067	invertible elements of $B$, then $\Omega_{B/A} = \Omega_{B/S^{-1}A}$.
34068	\item If $S \subset B$ is a multiplicative subset then
34069	$S^{-1}\Omega_{B/A} = \Omega_{S^{-1}B/A}$.
34070	\end{enumerate}
34071	\end{lemma}
34072	
34073	\begin{proof}
34074	To show the equality of (1) it is enough to show that any
34075	$A$-derivation $D : B \to M$ annihilates the elements $\varphi(s)^{-1}$.
34076	This is clear from the Leibniz rule applied to
34077	$1 = \varphi(s) \varphi(s)^{-1}$.
34078	To show (2) note that there is an obvious map
34079	$S^{-1}\Omega_{B/A} \to \Omega_{S^{-1}B/A}$.
34080	To show it is an isomorphism it is enough to show that
34081	there is a $A$-derivation $\text{d}'$ of $S^{-1}B$ into $S^{-1}\Omega_{B/A}$.
34082	To define it we simply set
34083	$\text{d}'(b/s) = (1/s)\text{d}b - (1/s^2)b\text{d}s$.
34084	Details omitted.
34085	\end{proof}

34087	\begin{lemma}
34088	\label{lemma-differential-seq}
34089	In diagram (\ref{equation-functorial-omega}),
34090	suppose that $S \to S'$ is surjective with kernel $I \subset S$,
34091	and assume that $R' = R$.
34092	Then there is a canonical exact sequence of $S'$-modules
34093	$$
34094	I/I^2
34095	\longrightarrow
34096	\Omega_{S/R} \otimes_S S'
34097	\longrightarrow
34098	\Omega_{S'/R}
34099	\longrightarrow
34100	0
34101	$$
34102	The leftmost map is characterized by the rule that
34103	$f \in I$ maps to $\text{d}f \otimes 1$.
34104	\end{lemma}
34105	
34106	\begin{proof}
34107	The middle term is $\Omega_{S/R} \otimes_S S/I$.
34108	For $f \in I$ denote $\overline{f}$ the image of $f$ in $I/I^2$.
34109	To show that the map $\overline{f} \mapsto \text{d}f \otimes 1$
34110	is well defined we just have to check that
34111	$\text{d} f_1f_2 \otimes 1 = 0$ if $f_1, f_2 \in I$.
34112	And this is clear from the Leibniz rule
34113	$\text{d} f_1f_2 \otimes 1 = (f_1 \text{d}f_2 + f_2 \text{d} f_1 )\otimes 1 =
34114	\text{d}f_2 \otimes f_1 + \text{d}f_1 \otimes f_2 = 0$.
34115	A similar computation show this map is $S' = S/I$-linear.
34116	
34117	\medskip\noindent
34118	The map $\Omega_{S/R} \otimes_S S' \to \Omega_{S'/R}$
34119	is the canonical $S'$-linear map associated to the
34120	$S$-linear map $\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S'/R}$.
34121	It is surjective because $\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S'/R}$
34122	is surjective by Lemma \ref{lemma-differential-surjective}.
34123	
34124	\medskip\noindent
34125	The composite of the two maps is zero because
34126	$\text{d}f$ maps to zero in $\Omega_{S'/R}$
34127	for $f \in I$. Note that exactness just says that
34128	the kernel of $\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S'/R}$
34129	is generated as an $S$-submodule by the submodule $I\Omega_{S/R}$ together
34130	with the elements $\text{d}f$, with $f \in I$. We know by
34131	Lemma \ref{lemma-differential-surjective}
34132	that this kernel is generated by the elements $\text{d}(a)$
34133	where $\varphi(a) = \beta(r)$ for some $r \in R$.
34134	But then $a = \alpha(r) + a - \alpha(r)$, so
34135	$\text{d}(a) = \text{d}(a - \alpha(r))$. And
34136	$a - \alpha(r) \in I$ since $\varphi(a - \alpha(r)) =
34137	\varphi(a) - \varphi(\alpha(r)) = \beta(r) - \beta(r) = 0$.
34138	We conclude the elements $\text{d}f$ with $f \in I$ already
34139	generate the kernel as an $S$-module, as desired.
34140	\end{proof}

34144	In diagram (\ref{equation-functorial-omega}),

34146	and assume that $R' = R$. Moreover, assume that there exists

37042	
37043	\noindent
37044	Let us motivate the definition of a smooth ring map by an example.
37045	Suppose $R$ is a ring and $S = R[x, y]/(f)$ for some nonzero $f \in R[x, y]$.
37046	In this case there is an exact sequence
37047	$$
37048	S \to
37049	S\text{d}x \oplus S\text{d}y \to
37050	\Omega_{S/R} \to 0
37051	$$
37052	where the first arrow maps $1$ to
37053	$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} \text{d}x +
37054	\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} \text{d}y$ see
37055	Section \ref{section-netherlander}.
37056	We conclude that $\Omega_{S/R}$ is locally free of rank $1$ if
37057	the partial derivatives of $f$ generate the unit ideal in $S$.
37058	In this case $S$ is smooth of relative dimension $1$ over $R$.
37059	But it can happen that $\Omega_{S/R}$ is locally free of rank $2$
37060	namely if both partial derivatives of $f$ are zero. For example if
37061	for a prime $p$ we have $p = 0$ in $R$ and $f = x^p + y^p$ then this
37062	happens. Here $R \to S$ is a relative global complete intersection
37063	of relative dimension $1$ which is not smooth.
37064	Hence, in order to check that a ring map
37065	is smooth it is not sufficient to check whether the module of differentials
37066	is free. The correct condition is the following.
37067	
37068	\begin{definition}
37069	\label{definition-smooth}
37070	A ring map $R \to S$ is {\it smooth} if it is of finite presentation
37071	and the naive cotangent complex $\NL_{S/R}$ is quasi-isomorphic to a
37072	finite projective $S$-module placed in degree $0$: this means
37073	that $H_1(\NL_{S/R}) = 0$ and that $\Omega_{S/R}$ is a finite projective
37074	$S$-module.
37075	\end{definition}
37076	
37077	\noindent
37078	Let $R \to S$ be a ring map. By Lemma \ref{lemma-NL-homotopy} for any
37079	presentation $\alpha : P \to S$ we have $H_1(\NL(\alpha)) = H_1(\NL_{S/R})$
37080	and $H_0(\NL(\alpha)) = \Omega_{S/R}$. Thus, if $R \to S$ is smooth,
37081	then for any surjection $\alpha : R[x_1, \ldots, x_n] \to S$ with kernel

37091	as $S$-modules and $I/I^2$ is a finite projective

\subsection{Stage 6 sub-decomposition: 00OE + 02JK chain (iter-198)}
\label{subsec:stage6_subgap_decomposition}

Stage 6 is the residual gap inside
\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}'s six-stage chain: given Stages
1--5b (a standard-smooth presentation of an affine chart
\(R \to S\) at a point \(z\), with \(\Omega_{S/R}\) free of rank \(n\)
and \(\Omega_{S_\mathfrak q / R}\) free of rank \(n\) on the stalk
\(S_\mathfrak q = \mathcal O_{X, z}\)), one must conclude that
\(S_\mathfrak q\) is a regular local ring. The Stacks proof of tag
\texttt{00TT} ultimately reduces to two ingredients which Mathlib does
not yet ship at commit \texttt{b80f227}: (6.A) the smooth-algebra
Krull-dimension formula (Stacks tag \texttt{00OE}, in the algebra
chapter as Lemma~\texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}),
and (6.B) the cotangent / K\"ahler-differential bridge over a field
base (Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}, in the algebra chapter as
Lemma~\texttt{lemma-differential-seq} plus the separable-residue-field
refinement of Lemma~\texttt{lemma-separable-smooth}). The two
ingredients combine (6.C) into the regularity conclusion via
\(\dim S_\mathfrak q = \mathrm{rank}_{S_\mathfrak q}\Omega_{S_\mathfrak
q / R} = \dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\),
which is the cotangent-space characterisation of regularity that
Mathlib's \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.iff\_finrank\_cotangentSpace}
already exposes. The next three lemmas package the three sub-gaps as
named iter-199+ prover targets.

\begin{lemma}
  [Stage 6.A --- Smooth-algebra Krull-dimension formula (Stacks 00OE)]
  \label{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}
  \lean{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.ringKrullDim_localization_eq_relativeDimension}
  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field
  % (canonically known as tag 00OE; tag-to-label cross-check not in the
  % local `tags/tags` file, see iter-198 writer report note) (read from
  % references/stacks-algebra.tex, L28206--28217 in section
  % "Dimension theory of finitely generated $k$-algebras").
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $k$ be a field. Let $S$ be a finite type $k$
  % algebra. Let $X = \Spec(S)$. Let $\mathfrak p \subset S$ be a prime
  % ideal, and let $x \in X$ be the corresponding point. Then we have
  % $\dim_x(X) = \dim(S_{\mathfrak p}) + \text{trdeg}_k\
  % \kappa(\mathfrak p)$."
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~00OE (\(=\) \texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}).}
  Let \(k\) be a field, let \(S\) be a finite-type \(k\)-algebra, and let
  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\) be a prime ideal corresponding to a point
  \(x \in \mathrm{Spec}(S)\). Then
  \[
    \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)
    \;=\; \dim S_{\mathfrak q} + \mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q).
  \]
  In particular, if \(\mathfrak q\) is a maximal ideal (so \(x\) is a
  closed point) and \(k\) is algebraically closed, then
  \(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = k\) (Nullstellensatz), so
  \(\mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q) = 0\) and
  \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q} = \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)\).

  \paragraph{Geometric application.} Applied to the iter-193 Stage~3
  setup --- \(k = \bar k\), \(S = \Gamma(X.\mathrm{left}, V)\) a
  standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra of relative dimension \(n\) (Stage
  3), and \(\mathfrak q\) the prime defining the point \(z \in V\) --- the
  formula yields \(\dim(\mathcal O_{X, z}) = \dim S_{\mathfrak q} = n -
  \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(z)\). For \(z\) a closed point this is
  simply \(n\); for the generic point of a codim-\(1\) subvariety
  (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) it is \(1\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}
  [Stage 6.B --- Cotangent \(\leftrightarrow\) K\"ahler over a field (Stacks 02JK)]
  \label{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field}
  \lean{Algebra.KaehlerDifferential.cotangent_iso_residue_tensor_kaehler}
  \uses{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], lemma-differential-seq (canonically
  % known as the right-exact conormal portion of tag 02JK; the
  % short-exact / split refinement is the proof step inside
  % lemma-separable-smooth) (read from
  % references/stacks-algebra.tex, L34087--34104 and L38724--38766).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "In diagram (\ref{equation-functorial-omega}),
  % suppose that $S \to S'$ is surjective with kernel $I \subset S$, and
  % assume that $R' = R$. Then there is a canonical exact sequence of
  % $S'$-modules
  % $$I/I^2 \longrightarrow \Omega_{S/R} \otimes_S S' \longrightarrow
  % \Omega_{S'/R} \longrightarrow 0$$
  % The leftmost map is characterized by the rule that $f \in I$ maps
  % to $\text{d}f \otimes 1$."
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Hence we get
  % $$\dim_{\kappa} \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa = \dim_\kappa
  % \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 + \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa) \geq \dim R +
  % \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa) = \dim_{\mathfrak q} S$$
  % (see Lemma \ref{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field} for
  % the last equality) with equality if and only if $R$ is regular."
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~02JK (right-exact half = \texttt{lemma-differential-seq}; separable-residue refinement inside \texttt{lemma-separable-smooth}).}
  Let \(k\) be a field and let \(R\) be a Noetherian local
  \(k\)-algebra with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\) and residue field
  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\). The canonical exact sequence
  \[
    \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa
    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{\kappa / k}
    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
  \]
  identifies the cotangent space \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) (a
  \(\kappa\)-vector space) with the kernel of the right map. When
  \(\kappa / k\) is separable (in particular when \(k\) is
  algebraically closed and \(\kappa = k\), so \(\Omega_{\kappa / k}
  = 0\)) the leftmost map is injective and the sequence is short exact
  --- in fact split short exact when there is a \(k\)-algebra section
  \(\kappa \to R\) (e.g.\ at a closed point of a \(\bar k\)-variety).
  In particular, over an algebraically closed field \(k = \bar k\) and
  at a closed point \(\mathfrak m\),
  \[
    \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa \;\simeq\; \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
  \]
  as \(\kappa\)-vector spaces; hence
  \(\dim_\kappa(\Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa)
   = \dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}
  [Stage 6.C --- Assembly: smooth standard-smooth stalk is regular local]
  \label{lem:stage6_regular_stalk_assembly}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isRegularLocalRing_stalk_of_smooth_aux}
  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim,
        lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula,
        lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field}
  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field, let
  \(R \to S\) be a standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra presentation of
  relative dimension \(n\) on an affine chart \(\Spec S \subseteq X\),
  and let \(z \in \Spec S\) be a point corresponding to a prime
  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\). Then the stalk \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) is a
  regular local ring of Krull dimension
  \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\). In the
  closed-point case (\(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = \bar k\),
  \(\mathrm{trdeg} = 0\)) the dimension equals \(n\); in the
  codim-\(1\) generic-point case (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) the
  dimension equals \(1\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  Combine the three sub-lemmas:
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item By \cref{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim} (Stage~5b)
      the localised K\"ahler module \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q} / R}\)
      is free of rank \(n\) over \(S_{\mathfrak q}\).
    \item By \cref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula} (Stage 6.A,
      Stacks 00OE) the Krull dimension of \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) equals
      \(\dim_z \Spec S - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\),
      which for a standard-smooth presentation of relative dimension
      \(n\) equals \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\).
      (For the standard-smooth presentation the dimension
      \(\dim_z \Spec S = n\) follows from the global-complete-intersection
      structure: \(n\) variables modulo \(n - \dim\) equations, see
      Stacks \texttt{lemma-relative-global-complete-intersection-smooth}.)
    \item By \cref{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field} (Stage 6.B,
      Stacks 02JK) the residue-field tensor \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q}/R}
      \otimes_{S_{\mathfrak q}} \kappa(\mathfrak q)\) is isomorphic to
      \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) as \(\kappa(\mathfrak q)\)-vector
      spaces. Combined with (1), \(\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak
      m / \mathfrak m^2 = n\) at the closed-point case (and in general
      \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\) by base-change
      of the standard-smooth presentation to the closed-point fibre).
    \item Compare (2) and (3): both \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q}\) and
      \(\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) equal
      \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\); the
      regularity criterion for Noetherian local rings (the equality
      \(\dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2 = \dim R\) is the
      defining condition for a regular local ring, Stacks
      \texttt{definition-regular-local} \(=\) Mathlib
      \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing}) then gives \(S_{\mathfrak q}\)
      regular. The scheme-level transfer
      \(S_{\mathfrak q} = \mathcal O_{X, z}\) (Stage 3
      \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime} on the affine chart) completes
      the conclusion.
  \end{enumerate}
\end{proof}

pieces are needed to formalise Stages 6.A--6.C; status as audited iter-198.

299	\begin{lemma}
300	  [Stage 6.A --- Smooth-algebra Krull-dimension formula (Stacks 00OE)]
301	  \label{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}
302	  \lean{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.ringKrullDim_localization_eq_relativeDimension}
303	  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}
304	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field
305	  % (canonically known as tag 00OE; tag-to-label cross-check not in the
306	  % local `tags/tags` file, see iter-198 writer report note) (read from
307	  % references/stacks-algebra.tex, L28206--28217 in section
308	  % "Dimension theory of finitely generated $k$-algebras").
309	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $k$ be a field. Let $S$ be a finite type $k$
310	  % algebra. Let $X = \Spec(S)$. Let $\mathfrak p \subset S$ be a prime
311	  % ideal, and let $x \in X$ be the corresponding point. Then we have
312	  % $\dim_x(X) = \dim(S_{\mathfrak p}) + \text{trdeg}_k\
313	  % \kappa(\mathfrak p)$."
314	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~00OE (\(=\) \texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}).}
315	  Let \(k\) be a field, let \(S\) be a finite-type \(k\)-algebra, and let
316	  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\) be a prime ideal corresponding to a point
317	  \(x \in \mathrm{Spec}(S)\). Then
318	  \[
319	    \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)
320	    \;=\; \dim S_{\mathfrak q} + \mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q).
321	  \]
322	  In particular, if \(\mathfrak q\) is a maximal ideal (so \(x\) is a
323	  closed point) and \(k\) is algebraically closed, then
324	  \(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = k\) (Nullstellensatz), so
325	  \(\mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q) = 0\) and
326	  \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q} = \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)\).
327	
328	  \paragraph{Geometric application.} Applied to the iter-193 Stage~3
329	  setup --- \(k = \bar k\), \(S = \Gamma(X.\mathrm{left}, V)\) a
330	  standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra of relative dimension \(n\) (Stage
331	  3), and \(\mathfrak q\) the prime defining the point \(z \in V\) --- the
332	  formula yields \(\dim(\mathcal O_{X, z}) = \dim S_{\mathfrak q} = n -
333	  \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(z)\). For \(z\) a closed point this is
334	  simply \(n\); for the generic point of a codim-\(1\) subvariety
335	  (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) it is \(1\).
336	\end{lemma}

338	\begin{lemma}
339	  [Stage 6.B --- Cotangent \(\leftrightarrow\) K\"ahler over a field (Stacks 02JK)]
340	  \label{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field}
341	  \lean{Algebra.KaehlerDifferential.cotangent_iso_residue_tensor_kaehler}
342	  \uses{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}
343	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], lemma-differential-seq (canonically
344	  % known as the right-exact conormal portion of tag 02JK; the
345	  % short-exact / split refinement is the proof step inside
346	  % lemma-separable-smooth) (read from
347	  % references/stacks-algebra.tex, L34087--34104 and L38724--38766).
348	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "In diagram (\ref{equation-functorial-omega}),
349	  % suppose that $S \to S'$ is surjective with kernel $I \subset S$, and
350	  % assume that $R' = R$. Then there is a canonical exact sequence of
351	  % $S'$-modules
352	  % $$I/I^2 \longrightarrow \Omega_{S/R} \otimes_S S' \longrightarrow
353	  % \Omega_{S'/R} \longrightarrow 0$$
354	  % The leftmost map is characterized by the rule that $f \in I$ maps
355	  % to $\text{d}f \otimes 1$."
356	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Hence we get
357	  % $$\dim_{\kappa} \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa = \dim_\kappa
358	  % \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 + \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa) \geq \dim R +
359	  % \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa) = \dim_{\mathfrak q} S$$
360	  % (see Lemma \ref{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field} for
361	  % the last equality) with equality if and only if $R$ is regular."
362	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~02JK (right-exact half = \texttt{lemma-differential-seq}; separable-residue refinement inside \texttt{lemma-separable-smooth}).}
363	  Let \(k\) be a field and let \(R\) be a Noetherian local
364	  \(k\)-algebra with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\) and residue field
365	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\). The canonical exact sequence
366	  \[
367	    \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
368	    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa
369	    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{\kappa / k}

370	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
371	  \]
372	  identifies the cotangent space \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) (a
373	  \(\kappa\)-vector space) with the kernel of the right map. When
374	  \(\kappa / k\) is separable (in particular when \(k\) is
375	  algebraically closed and \(\kappa = k\), so \(\Omega_{\kappa / k}
376	  = 0\)) the leftmost map is injective and the sequence is short exact
377	  --- in fact split short exact when there is a \(k\)-algebra section
378	  \(\kappa \to R\) (e.g.\ at a closed point of a \(\bar k\)-variety).
379	  In particular, over an algebraically closed field \(k = \bar k\) and
380	  at a closed point \(\mathfrak m\),
381	  \[
382	    \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa \;\simeq\; \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
383	  \]
384	  as \(\kappa\)-vector spaces; hence
385	  \(\dim_\kappa(\Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa)
386	   = \dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\).
387	\end{lemma}

389	\begin{lemma}
390	  [Stage 6.C --- Assembly: smooth standard-smooth stalk is regular local]
391	  \label{lem:stage6_regular_stalk_assembly}
392	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isRegularLocalRing_stalk_of_smooth_aux}
393	  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim,
394	        lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula,
395	        lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field}
396	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field, let
397	  \(R \to S\) be a standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra presentation of
398	  relative dimension \(n\) on an affine chart \(\Spec S \subseteq X\),
399	  and let \(z \in \Spec S\) be a point corresponding to a prime
400	  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\). Then the stalk \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) is a
401	  regular local ring of Krull dimension
402	  \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\). In the
403	  closed-point case (\(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = \bar k\),
404	  \(\mathrm{trdeg} = 0\)) the dimension equals \(n\); in the
405	  codim-\(1\) generic-point case (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) the
406	  dimension equals \(1\).
407	\end{lemma}
408	
409	\begin{proof}
410	  Combine the three sub-lemmas:
411	  \begin{enumerate}
412	    \item By \cref{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim} (Stage~5b)
413	      the localised K\"ahler module \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q} / R}\)
414	      is free of rank \(n\) over \(S_{\mathfrak q}\).
415	    \item By \cref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula} (Stage 6.A,
416	      Stacks 00OE) the Krull dimension of \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) equals
417	      \(\dim_z \Spec S - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\),
418	      which for a standard-smooth presentation of relative dimension
419	      \(n\) equals \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\).
420	      (For the standard-smooth presentation the dimension
421	      \(\dim_z \Spec S = n\) follows from the global-complete-intersection
422	      structure: \(n\) variables modulo \(n - \dim\) equations, see
423	      Stacks \texttt{lemma-relative-global-complete-intersection-smooth}.)
424	    \item By \cref{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field} (Stage 6.B,
425	      Stacks 02JK) the residue-field tensor \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q}/R}
426	      \otimes_{S_{\mathfrak q}} \kappa(\mathfrak q)\) is isomorphic to
427	      \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) as \(\kappa(\mathfrak q)\)-vector
428	      spaces. Combined with (1), \(\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak
429	      m / \mathfrak m^2 = n\) at the closed-point case (and in general
430	      \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\) by base-change
431	      of the standard-smooth presentation to the closed-point fibre).
432	    \item Compare (2) and (3): both \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q}\) and
433	      \(\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) equal
434	      \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\); the
435	      regularity criterion for Noetherian local rings (the equality
436	      \(\dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2 = \dim R\) is the
437	      defining condition for a regular local ring, Stacks
438	      \texttt{definition-regular-local} \(=\) Mathlib
439	      \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing}) then gives \(S_{\mathfrak q}\)
440	      regular. The scheme-level transfer
441	      \(S_{\mathfrak q} = \mathcal O_{X, z}\) (Stage 3
442	      \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime} on the affine chart) completes
443	      the conclusion.
444	  \end{enumerate}
445	\end{proof}
